figures.caption: Fig. 4: Qualitative results on text-to-image synthesis.
Table 7: Quantitative comparison on ImageNet 256× 256 on SiT-XL.

Fig. 8: Qualitative results on CIFAR-10. † denotes a base solver with an increased NFEs to match the wall-clock time of our framework. Compared to the computematched baseline(†), FDS effectively enhances the generation quality.
Fig. 12: Qualitative results for text-to-image generation.

Fig. 11: Qualitative results on ImageNet 256× 256. Generated samples using the SiT-XL backbone. Arranged from top to bottom, every two rows show instances from classes 1 (goldfish), 140 (dunlin), 483 (castle), and 933 (cheeseburger).

Table 1: Performance comparison on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet 256 × 256. FDS consistently improves generation quality in terms of FID across all configurations in the main experiments. † denotes a base solver with an increased NFEs to match the wall-clock time of our framework.
Fig. 3: Qualitative results on ImageNet 256× 256 with JiT-L/16. Compared to the compute-matched baseline(†), FDS effectively enhances the generation quality.
Fig. 7: Effect of iterations N and candidates M . While increasing N and M generally improves performance, setting N = M = 1 offers the best trade-off.
Table 5: Throughput across different configurations. Values indicate the wallclock generation time, measured in seconds per batch (sec./batch).

Fig. 10: Qualitative results on ImageNet 256× 256. Generated samples using the JiT-L/16 backbone. Arranged from top to bottom, every two rows show instances from classes 1 (goldfish), 140 (dunlin), 483 (castle), and 933 (cheeseburger).
Fig. 6: Ablation on early-stage refinement. Applying FDS during earlier denoising stages effectively improves generation quality. Bold text indicates the default configuration.

Fig. 2: 2D Synthetic Experiment shows our divergence-based criterion correlates with sample quality. (a) FDS achieves more accurate modeling of the target distribution than standard FM, yielding a lower Wasserstein Distance (WD). (b) Standard FM passes xtk directly to the ODE solver, whereas FDS refines xtk into x̃tk , moving it to a low-divergence region. (c) Discrepancy maps computed from the ground-truth samplewise velocities (Top) and from our inference-time surrogate using the pre-trained model (Bottom) are highly consistent.

Fig. 9: Qualitative results on ImageNet 256× 256. Generated samples using the JiT-B/16 backbone. Arranged from top to bottom, every two rows show instances from classes 1 (goldfish), 140 (dunlin), 483 (castle), and 933 (cheeseburger).
Table 6: Quantitative comparison on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet 256 × 256. Results are evaluated using 100 denoising steps.
Fig. 13: Qualitative results on Gaussian deblurring.
